script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Tuesday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed four point six zero percent down on the session.

Marvell Technology led all primary holdings lower on Tuesday, shedding seven point seven five percent. That kind of single-session drawdown in a top-ten weight demands an explanation, and the sector is not offering an easy one.

Chip stocks fell broadly on Tuesday as geopolitical pressure returned to the foreground. Middle East tensions sent oil prices sharply higher, weighing on risk appetite across technology and growth names. No major domestic macro print drove the selloff, but the risk-off rotation was visible across the tape, with the Nasdaq finishing the session sharply lower in sympathy with the semiconductor complex.

The most consequential development for this portfolio centers on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, and it connects directly to Advanced Micro Devices, Nvidia, and the broader AI hardware thesis. A widely circulated report confirmed that TSMC posted revenue growth of thirty-six percent in its latest quarter — a blowout result that underscores the durability of AI-driven chip demand at the foundry level. The catch: memory stocks cratered on the same session, signaling that the market is drawing a sharp line between foundry capacity, which remains stressed and profitable, and memory supply, which may be showing early signs of demand softness. For holders of both Micron and the AI logic names, that divergence is worth watching closely. TSMC's earnings call is expected to provide further detail on capacity allocation and regional exposure in the sessions ahead.

Advanced Micro Devices was down four point two one percent on the session. The stock's move largely tracked the sector, though one report noted the broader chip trade is waiting on TSMC's full earnings disclosure for directional clarity on AI hardware demand. Watch that foundry readout closely as a near-term catalyst.

Micron Technology was down four point three two percent. The most relevant pressure point for Micron is the ASML earnings report due Wednesday. ASML, which supplies critical chip manufacturing equipment, is expected to address capacity outlook and its exposure to proposed U.S. export control legislation that would require allied nations to align with restrictions on China sales. ASML has publicly stated it does not sell its most advanced extreme ultraviolet tools to China, but less-advanced deep ultraviolet tools represent up to twenty percent of ASML's projected annual revenue this year. Any guidance revision or commentary on the legislative risk could ripple directly into memory and equipment names across this portfolio.

Nvidia was down three point five two percent on the session, making it the relative outperformer among the largest holdings on Tuesday. No Nvidia-specific catalyst drove the move. The stock's relative resilience may reflect continued confidence in its data center franchise ahead of upcoming earnings.

Broadcom was down three point nine eight percent. The stock continues to carry a structural positive in the form of its extended Apple custom chip agreement running through twenty thirty-one, covering multiple generations of Apple silicon products. That long-dated contract anchors Broadcom's visibility in the custom accelerator market and supports its position as a key infrastructure supplier to major cloud and AI customers.

Intel was down six point one two percent, the second-largest single-session decline among primary holdings on Tuesday. No Intel-specific headline drove the move. The stock's deeper drawdown relative to peers reflects ongoing investor skepticism around its foundry transition and competitive positioning in the AI accelerator space. Watch for any manufacturing update as a potential re-rating trigger.

Applied Materials was down four point five zero percent on the session. As a semiconductor equipment supplier, Applied Materials faces the same ASML-adjacent headline risk around export controls and China demand. Wednesday's ASML report will set near-term tone for the entire equipment group.

KLA Corporation was down four point zero zero percent. KLA tracks closely with the equipment complex and faces the same China exposure narrative. No KLA-specific news crossed Tuesday.

Turning to Marvell Technology — down seven point seven five percent, the steepest single-session loss in the portfolio on Tuesday. No Marvell-specific news drove the decline, which suggests the move was largely technical and sentiment-driven within the broader chip selloff. The magnitude warrants monitoring. Any stabilization or analyst response in the next session would be a meaningful signal.

Lam Research was down five point eight three percent on Tuesday. Like Applied Materials and KLA, Lam sits squarely in the equipment subsector facing China export control uncertainty. The ASML print Wednesday effectively serves as a proxy read for the entire equipment group.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company was down two point eight nine percent, the smallest loss among all primary holdings on Tuesday, even as sector-wide pressure mounted. The strong revenue growth figure reported earlier this week continues to provide a fundamental floor for sentiment on the stock.

Three events to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First: ASML quarterly earnings, due Wednesday morning — the single most important near-term read for semiconductor equipment names including Applied Materials, Lam Research, and KLA. Second: any Congressional or executive branch commentary on the proposed export control alignment legislation, which names ASML directly and carries implications for China-exposed chip names. Third: TSMC's full earnings call, expected Wednesday, which will provide granular guidance on AI chip capacity allocation and regional demand trends directly relevant to Advanced Micro Devices and Nvidia.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Wednesday centers on ASML — what they say about China, capacity, and export controls will set the tone for the entire semiconductor complex.
